questions.question: How is anxiety best described in this lesson?

options.text: A character flaw that indicates weakness
Your body's alarm system designed to keep you safe
A rare condition that affects very few people
Something you can simply think your way out of

questions.explanation: Anxiety is your body's alarm system -- not a character flaw. Like a car alarm, it is designed to protect you, but sometimes it becomes oversensitive and goes off when there is no real danger.

questions.question: What are the three areas where anxiety shows up?

options.text: Past, present, and future
Thoughts, body, and actions
Home, work, and school
Morning, afternoon, and night

questions.explanation: Anxiety affects three key areas: thoughts (worrying, 'what if' scenarios), body (racing heart, muscle tension, shallow breathing), and actions (avoiding places, canceling plans, checking things repeatedly).

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'problem anxiety' versus normal anxiety?

options.text: It only occurs during genuinely dangerous situations
It fades quickly after the stressful event ends
It shows up when you are actually safe and makes you avoid things that matter
It helps you perform better under pressure

questions.explanation: Problem anxiety is different from normal anxiety because it shows up when you are actually safe, is too intense for the situation, sticks around too long, and makes you avoid things that matter.

questions.question: Approximately how many adults are affected by anxiety?

options.text: About 5 million
About 10 million
More than 40 million
About 100 million

questions.explanation: Anxiety affects more than 40 million adults. It is one of the most common mental health experiences and one of the most treatable. You are not alone.

questions.question: Why is 'just think positive' harmful advice for someone with anxiety?

options.text: Because positive thinking is always unhelpful
Because anxiety involves complex brain chemistry and nervous system responses, not just thinking patterns
Because people with anxiety cannot think at all
Because positive thinking makes anxiety worse

questions.explanation: Anxiety is not simply a thinking problem. It involves complex brain chemistry and nervous system responses. Telling someone to 'just think positive' dismisses the real, often physical nature of the condition.

title: Understanding Your Anxiety: Your Body's Alarm System - Quiz
